---
title: "Modeling Antibody Kinetics Following Shigella Infection Using a Two-Phase Bayesian Hierarchical Framework"
author:
  - name: "Kwan Ho Lee"
    affiliation: "Department of Public Health Sciences, University of California Davis School of Medicine"
    corresponding: true
    email: ksjlee@ucdavis.edu
date: today
---

# Methods

## Study Design

We conducted a longitudinal observational study to characterize within-host antibody kinetics following confirmed *Shigella* infection. Repeated serological measurements were collected from laboratory-confirmed cases and analyzed using a two-phase within-host antibody model within a Bayesian hierarchical framework. 
The objective was to estimate antigen- and isotype-specific rise, peak, and decay parameters while quantifying inter-individual variability in antibody responses.

## Study Setting and Data Sources

Longitudinal case data were obtained from pediatric and adult patients presenting with acute diarrhea in Dhaka, Bangladesh. Enrollment and sample collection occurred between March 2021 and 2022 through hospital- and field-based surveillance conducted by icddr,b.

In addition to the longitudinal case data, cross-sectional serological datasets from Ghana, Niger, Sierra Leone, and other sites were available from population-based studies conducted between 2016 and 2020. 
These cross-sectional datasets were not analyzed in the present study but informed assay interpretation and will be used in subsequent seroincidence analyses.

## Participants

### Longitudinal Shigella Cases

Inclusion criteria were individuals presenting with acute diarrhea (duration \<72 hours), frequent bowel movements (\>3 in 24 hours), and laboratory confirmation of *Shigella* infection by culture and/or molecular diagnostic assays (PCR or rapid PCR). Exclusion criteria were inability to provide a blood sample.

A total of 48 individuals with confirmed *Shigella* infection were included. Serum or plasma samples were collected at baseline (acute presentation) and approximately 7, 30, 90, and 180 days following infection.

The distribution of infecting serotypes was not uniform. Most cases were infected with *Shigella flexneri* 2a, with smaller numbers of *S. flexneri* 3a, *S. flexneri* 6, *S. sonnei*, and other serotypes. 
This imbalance is relevant for interpretation of O-antigen–specific responses due to known cross-reactivity between *S. flexneri* 2a and 3a, and minimal cross-reactivity between *S. sonnei* and *S. flexneri* 6.

## Laboratory Measurements

IgG and IgA antibody responses were measured using a multiplex bead-based immunoassay. Antigens included:

-   Invasion plasmid antigen B (IpaB), a conserved virulence protein shared across *Shigella* species and other *Salmonella*.
-   O-specific polysaccharides (OSP) from the most prevalent *Shigella* serotypes globally: *S. sonnei*, *S. flexneri* 2a, *S. flexneri* 3a, and *S. flexneri* 6.

Assay output was reported as median fluorescence intensity (MFI), providing semi-quantitative measures of antigen-specific antibody concentrations. 
Serum or plasma samples were derived from venous blood; dried blood spot samples were derived from capillary blood collected on filter paper.

## Variables

The primary outcome was the longitudinal antibody concentration, measured as median fluorescence intensity (MFI), for each antigen–isotype combination (IgG and IgA). 
Time since infection was measured in days relative to the baseline sample collected at acute presentation.

Key model-derived parameters included baseline antibody level, time to peak response, peak antibody concentration, decay rate, and decay shape.

Participant age at infection was recorded and used for stratified visualization of antibody trajectories. 
Age strata were defined as \<5 years, 5–15 years, and \>15 years, reflecting known differences in antibody kinetics between young children and older individuals.
Infecting serotype was also recorded and used for stratified visualization. 
Age and serotype were not included as covariates in the primary kinetic model.

## Data Preparation

Antibody measurements were log-transformed prior to modeling to stabilize variance and improve numerical stability. 
Time was expressed in days since baseline sampling.

Analyses were restricted to homologous antigen–serotype pairs when modeling OSP responses (e.g., *S. flexneri* 2a OSP responses were modeled using only individuals infected with *S. flexneri* 2a or closely related serotypes) to reduce bias from cross-reactivity.

All data preprocessing, model fitting, and post-processing were conducted in R. Bayesian inference was implemented using JAGS, with reproducible workflows developed within the **serodynamics** package. 
Prior predictive checks and posterior predictive diagnostics were performed to assess model plausibility and convergence.

## Within-Host Antibody Kinetics Model

Longitudinal antibody responses following infection were described using a two-phase within-host model consisting of a rapid rise phase followed by a slower decay phase [@deGraaf2014_TwoPhaseModel; @Teunis2016_LinkingHeterogeneity]. 
This formulation is consistent with previously described models of serological responses to infection and captures the characteristic rise–peak–decay pattern observed in antibody measurements.

Let $y(t)$ denote the antibody concentration at time $t$ since infection. 
The model is defined as:

$$
y(t) =
\begin{cases}
y_0 \exp(\mu_y t), & t \le t_1 \\
y_1 \left( 1 + (\rho - 1)\alpha y_1^{\,\rho-1}(t - t_1) \right)^{-\frac{1}{(\rho-1)}}, & t > t_1
\end{cases}
$$

where $y_0$ is the baseline antibody concentration, $\mu_y$ is the rate of antibody increase during the rise phase, $t_1$ is the time to peak response, $y_1$ is the peak antibody concentration, $\alpha$ is the decay rate, and $\rho$ is a shape parameter controlling the form of antibody waning. 
The decay phase reduces to exponential decay when $\rho = 1$, and allows for non-exponential waning when $\rho \neq 1$.

This closed-form representation corresponds to the solution of a two-phase within-host model in which antibody concentrations increase rapidly following infection and subsequently wane according to a power-law decay [@Teunis2016_LinkingHeterogeneity].

## Hierarchical Model Structure

To account for heterogeneity in antibody responses across individuals, model parameters were estimated within a hierarchical Bayesian framework. Individual-level kinetic parameters were assumed to arise from population-level distributions specific to each antigen–isotype combination.

For each individual $i$ and parameter $\theta \in \{ y_0, y_1, t_1, \alpha, \rho \}$, we assumed:

$$
\log(\theta_i) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta, \sigma_\theta^2)
$$

where $\mu_\theta$ and $\sigma_\theta$ represent population-level means and variances.
This partial pooling approach stabilizes inference for individuals with sparse measurements while preserving meaningful inter-individual variability.

Separate models were fit for each antigen–isotype combination.

### Observation Model

Observed antibody measurements were modeled on the log scale as noisy realizations of the latent two-phase antibody trajectory. For individual $i$ and antigen–isotype (biomarker) $j$ measured at time $t_{ij}$:

$$
\log\!\left(y^{\text{obs}}_{ij}\right) \sim 
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{\log y,ij},\ \tau_j^{-1}\right),
$$

where $\mu_{\log y,ij}$ denotes the expected log antibody level implied by the two-phase within-host model evaluated at time $t_{ij}$ using individual-level kinetic parameters, and $\tau_j$ is the measurement precision (inverse variance) specific to biomarker $j$.

## Prior Specification

Weakly informative priors were assigned to all population-level parameters to reflect plausible biological ranges while allowing the data to dominate inference. 
Prior distributions were scaled to the observed assay ranges for each antigen–isotype combination.

Priors on population-level means were specified on the log scale. 
Standard deviation parameters were assigned weakly informative priors constrained to be positive. 
These choices were informed by prior predictive simulations and existing applications of similar models to enteric pathogens.

## Model Fitting and Computation

Models were fit using Bayesian Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods implemented in JAGS and executed through R. 
Multiple chains were run with dispersed initial values. 
Convergence was assessed using visual inspection of trace plots and the potential scale reduction factor (R-hat) across all monitored parameters.

Posterior samples were retained after discarding burn-in iterations and thinning to reduce autocorrelation. 
All analyses were conducted using reproducible workflows implemented in the **serodynamics** R package.

## Model Diagnostics and Validation

Prior predictive checks were used to assess whether the specified priors generated biologically plausible antibody trajectories before conditioning on the observed data [@gelman2013bayesian; @gelman2020bayesian].

Model adequacy was evaluated using posterior predictive checks. 
Simulated antibody trajectories were compared to observed longitudinal data to assess whether the fitted model reproduced key features of the data, including peak timing, peak magnitude, and long-term decay patterns [@gelman2013bayesian].

Convergence diagnostics were examined for all model parameters. 
Individual-level posterior trajectories were visually inspected to confirm appropriate fit across participants with varying numbers of observations [@vehtari2021rank].

## Sensitivity Analyses

Sensitivity analyses were performed to assess robustness of inference to prior specification. 
Alternative weakly informative priors were considered for key population-level parameters, and resulting posterior estimates were compared for consistency.

Additional sensitivity analyses examined the influence of sparse individual trajectories by refitting models after excluding individuals with minimal follow-up.
Results were qualitatively unchanged, indicating that inferences were not driven by a small number of highly sampled individuals.

# Appendix

## Appendix A. Two-Phase Within-Host Model: Differential Equation Formulation

The closed-form antibody trajectories used in the main analysis arise from a two-phase within-host model describing antibody production and decay following infection.
Following @deGraaf2014_TwoPhaseModel and @Teunis2016_LinkingHeterogeneity, antibody concentration $y(t)$ is governed by:

$$
\frac{dy(t)}{dt} =
\begin{cases}
\mu_y\, y(t), & t \le t_1, \\
-\alpha\, y(t)^{\,\rho}, & t > t_1,
\end{cases}
$$

where $\mu$ denotes the antibody production rate during the rise phase, $\alpha$ is the decay rate, and $\rho$ controls the shape of antibody waning.

Initial conditions are given by $y(0) = y_0$. 
The transition time $t_1$ corresponds to the peak antibody response.

## Appendix B. Closed-Form Solutions

Solving the system above yields the closed-form antibody trajectory used in model fitting:

-   **Rise phase (**$t \le t_1$):

$$
y(t) = y_0 \exp(\mu_y t)
$$

-   **Decay phase (**$t > t_1$):

$$
y(t) = y_1 \left[ 1 + (\rho - 1)\alpha y_1^{\,\rho-1}(t - t_1) \right]^{-\frac{1}{(\rho-1)}},
$$

where $y_1 = y(t_1)$ is the peak antibody concentration.

When $\rho = 1$, the decay phase reduces to exponential waning.

## Appendix C. Hierarchical Parameterization

For each individual $i$ and biomarker $j$, latent kinetic parameters were defined as:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{ij} =
\begin{pmatrix}
\log(y_{0,ij}) \\
\log(y_{1,ij}) \\
\log(t_{1,ij}) \\
\log(\alpha_{ij}) \\
\log(\rho_{ij} - 1)
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

Individual-level parameters were assumed to arise from biomarker-specific population distributions:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_j, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j).
$$

This formulation ensures positivity constraints and enables partial pooling across individuals.

## Appendix D. Observation Model and Likelihood

For completeness, we restate the observation model underlying the main analysis.

Observed antibody measurements were modeled on the log scale as noisy realizations of the latent two-phase antibody trajectory:

$$
\log(y^{\text{obs}}_{ij}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\log y,ij}, \tau_j^{-1}),
$$

where $\mu_{\log y,ij} = \log(y_{ij}(t_{ij}))$ is the expected log antibody level implied by the two-phase within-host model evaluated at time $t_{ij}$ using individual-level kinetic parameters, and $\tau_j$ denotes biomarker-specific measurement precision.

Conditional on the latent antibody trajectories, observations were assumed independent across time points within individuals and across individuals.

## Appendix E. Prior and Hyperprior Distributions

### Measurement Error Precision

The precision of measurement error for each antigen–isotype (biomarker) $j$ was assigned a Gamma prior:

$$
\tau_j \sim \text{Gamma}(a_j, b_j),
$$

where $\tau_j$ denotes the precision (inverse variance) of log-scale measurement error, and $(a_j, b_j)$ are shape and rate hyperparameters chosen to induce biologically plausible variability.

### Population-Level Mean Parameters

For each biomarker $j$, the population-level mean vector of log-transformed kinetic parameters was assigned a multivariate normal hyperprior:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_j \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathrm{hyp},j}, \boldsymbol{\Omega}_{\mathrm{hyp},j}),
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathrm{hyp},j}$ represents the prior mean and $\boldsymbol{\Omega}_{\mathrm{hyp},j}$ is a diagonal covariance matrix encoding weak prior information about plausible parameter ranges.

### Between-Individual Variability

Between-individual variability in kinetic parameters was captured by a biomarker-specific covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j$. 
A Wishart prior was placed on the corresponding precision matrix:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1} \sim \mathcal{W}(\boldsymbol{\Omega}_j, \nu_j),
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Omega}_j$ is a scale matrix and $\nu_j$ denotes the degrees of freedom.

### Prior Predictive Assessment

Prior distributions were evaluated using prior predictive simulations to ensure that implied antibody trajectories were biologically plausible prior to conditioning on observed data.


\newpage

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
